mnemonics.title: INTERRUPT HANDLING STEPS
mnemonics.content: ISAVE: Interrupt Save State Vector Execute Restore - I(nterrupt) SAVE (registers/pc) Vector (fetch from table) Execute (ISR) Restore (state and return)

mnemonics.title: INTERRUPT TYPES
mnemonics.content: MHN: Maskable Hardware Non-maskable - Two main categories: Maskable (can be ignored) and Non-maskable (must be handled)

mnemonics.title: INTERRUPT LATENCY COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: DIP: Detection Processing Service - D(elay in) I(nterrupt) P(rocessing) = time to detect + time to process request + time to execute ISR

acronyms.usage: Hardware line used by devices to request CPU attention
Critical interrupt that cannot be ignored by the CPU
Code that executes when an interrupt is handled
Table containing addresses of ISRs for each interrupt type
Chip that manages multiple interrupt requests and priorities
Signal sent by CPU to acknowledge an interrupt request
Method for devices to access memory without CPU intervention

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a series of doors (devices) connected in a hallway. The person (CPU) entering from one end checks each door in order. The first door checked has highest priority because it gets attention first.
Think of a building directory (vector table) with room numbers (vectors). When a visitor arrives (interrupt), they directly go to their specific room instead of checking every room.
Like a hierarchy of importance: a fire alarm (high priority) can interrupt a phone call (lower priority), which can interrupt a conversation about the weather.
Polled: Security guard checking each camera one by one. Vectored: Camera that detects motion immediately calls the specific handler for that camera.

analogies.analogy: Interrupt is like a phone ringing when someone calls - you stop what you're doing to answer. Polling is like repeatedly checking your phone every few seconds to see if anyone called - wasteful and inefficient.
Like a receptionist in an office. Multiple people (devices) want attention; the receptionist (controller) prioritizes them and notifies you (CPU) when it's your turn.
Like an address on an envelope. The postal system (CPU) uses the address (vector) to deliver the letter (interrupt handling) to the correct destination (ISR).
Like using a bookmark while reading a book. You note where you stopped (save PC and registers) so you can return to the exact same spot after handling an interruption.
